_meta.purpose: Single source of truth for pages/scenario/scenario.html. Driven by render_scenario.py.
_meta.notes: Built from the old scenario page's gameplay sections (The Game / How to Play / Your Mission). Quick Reference inherits the still-canonical distance constants from the old highway-map page (the stale fuel/money/4-city-return-loop mechanics were dropped per the current game design).

card.subBanner: Deep in the Heart: A Lone Star Journey

intro_html: You’ve been hired as a Junior Developer at Bat City Collective. Your first client is All My Eggses Live in Texas, who’ve commissioned a promotional video game. This page is the game itself — what you’re building, how it plays, and the references you’ll need at the keyboard.

theGame.label: The Game
theGame.about_html:
Deep in the Heart: A Lone Star Journey is a text-based adventure game inspired by the classic Oregon Trail — but set in modern Texas. Players start in San Antonio and must navigate highways, face random hazards, answer Texas trivia, and hopefully make it to the beach at Corpus Christi or Galveston.

Why text-based? The retro console aesthetic is intentional — it justifies why we’re building this in Python. Oregon Trail didn’t need fancy graphics, and neither does this. The charm is in the writing, the choices, and the dad jokes.
whatYoureBuildingItems_html: Module 1: Character creation, destination selection, basic travel
Module 2: Loops for continuous travel, random hazards with D20 mechanics
Module 3: Functions for modular code, lists for tracking visited cities
Module 4: Files for saving/loading, dictionaries for city data

payoffCallout.title: The Big Payoff
payoffCallout.body_html: By Module 5, you’ll have built a complete, playable game — and learned Python along the way. That’s a portfolio piece you can actually show people.

howToPlay.label: How to Play
howToPlay.winCondition_html: Reach the beach! The game ends when you arrive at either Corpus Christi or Galveston. There’s no way to lose — the game just keeps going until you make it. Every city in Texas is a valid stop (All My Eggses has restaurants everywhere!), so there are no “bad” choices — just longer routes to the beach.
gameLoopSteps_html: City Phase — Choose Your Destination. You’re in a city. Look at the available routes and pick where you want to go next.
Trivia — Prove You Know Texas. Answer a trivia question about your current city. Correct? Head to your chosen destination. Wrong? GPS malfunction! You’re randomly sent somewhere else.
Travel — Roll the D20. Roll a 20-sided die. Your roll × 10 = miles traveled. Roll a 1? That’s 10 miles. Roll a 20? That’s 200 miles!
Check — Did You Arrive? Yes? If it’s a beach city, you WIN! Otherwise, return to Step 1. No? You haven’t gone far enough. Obstacle time…
Obstacle — Texas Has Other Plans. Face a random Texas hazard — flip a coin! HEADS? You escape! Roll again. TAILS? Lose a turn or go backwards, then roll again.
movementRules_html: Route Lock: Once you pick a destination, you’re committed. No changing your mind mid-highway. You must arrive at that city before you can choose a new destination.
No Rollover Miles: If you need 30 miles and roll 200, you still just arrive. Extra miles don’t carry over. Every city starts fresh at 0 miles.
Backwards Boundaries: If an obstacle sends you backwards, you can only go as far as the nearest city behind you. You can’t leapfrog past cities.
City Reset: Whenever you arrive at any city (forwards or backwards), your miles reset to zero and you enter the City Phase with a new trivia question.
howToPlay.whatWeTrackIntro_html: At the end of the game, you’ll see your trip stats:

whatWeTrack.label: Total Miles
whatWeTrack.desc: Traveled across all legs of the journey

whatWeTrack.label: Cities Visited
whatWeTrack.desc: All stops made on your road trip

whatWeTrack.label: Hazards Escaped
whatWeTrack.desc: How many times Texas let you off easy

closingMentor.name: Terrence “T-Bone” Booker
closingMentor.quote_html: “What do we not track? Money, fuel, health, time, or inventory. This ain’t that kind of game. Just you, the road, and the beach. Keep it simple.”

yourMission.label: Your Mission
yourMission.rhythmIntro_html: Each module follows a three-stage sprint cycle. This mirrors how real game studios work — and ensures you never start from scratch:

rows.goal: Create the foundation. Get the basic feature working.
Add complexity. Build on what you made.
Polish and ship. Complete the feature.

yourMission.keyInsight_html: The key insight: You’re not doing homework — you’re shipping a product. Each stage builds on the last. Each module adds features to the same game. By the end, you’ll have a portfolio piece you can show off.

closingMentor.name: Danny Cavanaugh
closingMentor.quote_html: “Before you build a castle, build a room. Before a room, build a wall. Before a wall, place one brick. That’s what BUILD is — placing your first bricks.”

quickReference.label: Quick Reference
quickReference.intro_html: These named constants show up in early-module labs. By Module 4, the game shifts to loading city data from text files at runtime — but for Modules 1–2 these are the values you’ll work with directly.
quickReference.code_html:
# Distance constants (in miles, from San Antonio)
SAT_TO_AUSTIN = 80
SAT_TO_CORPUS = 150
SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200
SAT_TO_DALLAS = 275

# Travel mechanic
MILES_PER_PIP = 10  # D20 roll × 10 = miles traveled
quickReference.closingNote_html: Beach destinations (Corpus Christi and Galveston) end the game. Galveston distance constant is introduced in the labs that need it.
